text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Emily Carter
Riverside Family Practice

12 Riverside Way, Greenwich, London, SE10 9AB
Dear Dr Emily Carter
Michael Turner, DOB: 14-03-1995, Hospital No: H8349201 NHS No. 7392018456
Flat 3, 27 Millbank Court, Deptford, London, SE8 4JP 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today following concerns raised by his family about nocturnal events and photosensitivity. His parents accompanied him and provided additional history. Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change. They report that the pattern remains similar over the past year despite adherence to medication.

He describes a clear sensitivity to flickering or bright light. Triggers include sunlight through trees when travelling by car, rapidly changing images on television or handheld devices, and bright supermarket lighting. He and his parents have noticed brief eye discomfort and a sense of rising internal tension immediately before some of his events in brightly lit environments. He now uses a cap outdoors and lowers screen brightness, which they feel has helped reduce brief absences, though not the nocturnal convulsions. There is no consistent association with intercurrent illness, sleep deprivation, or missed doses according to the family’s diary. No recent head injuries or infections were reported.

Current medication, as reported by the family and cross-checked with his repeat prescriptions: Levetiracetam 1500 mg b.d., Sodium Valproate 750 mg b.d., Clobazam 10 mg nocte with an additional 5 mg in the morning on school/work days, and Topiramate 50 mg b.d. He has tolerated this regimen reasonably well. They note mild word-finding difficulty and reduced appetite, but no rash, mood lability, or gastrointestinal upset. He is fully compliant as supervised by his parents, using a weekly pill organiser. Sleep routine is regular, and caffeine intake is minimal.

Examination in clinic today was unremarkable. He was alert and oriented with no focal neurological deficits. Gait was normal and there were no interictal myoclonic jerks observed under standard clinic lighting. Cardiovascular and general examination were within normal limits.

Given the photosensitivity described, I have advised continued practical avoidance strategies as a family-reported measure: sunglasses when outdoors in bright conditions, a peaked cap, minimising exposure to rapidly flickering visuals, setting screens to lower brightness with blue-light filters, and keeping a log of lighting conditions around events. I have arranged an EEG with photic stimulation to characterise any photoparoxysmal response and to guide future management. I have also requested an updated serum trough level for Valproate, U&Es, LFTs, bicarbonate, and Vitamin D, as well as a bone health review given long-term therapy. We will review the results in clinic and consider any adjustments in due course. No medication changes were made today pending the EEG.

He and his parents are aware of general safety advice, including supervision around water, avoidance of heights without protection, and taking breaks when exposed to bright or flickering light. They have contact details for our epilepsy specialist nurse and were encouraged to call if there is any sustained increase in events, clusters not settling, or any concerning adverse effects.

I will update you following the EEG with photic stimulation and blood results. In the interim, they will continue their current routine and maintain the seizure diary with notes on lighting conditions.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week

Although his seizures fluctuate, his parents consider him reasonably well controlled, he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic seizures during both day and night, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies: The letter states 'he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week'. This is a clear numeric frequency. Other seizure types are mentioned: 'drop attacks occurring in batches' and 'tonic seizures during both day and night' without numeric rates. Per the rule to select the highest frequency, compare known numeric frequency (3 per week) against others: since the others lack numeric frequency, they cannot be used to produce a higher quantified rate. Therefore, final label is 3 per week based on the explicit statement.
seizure_frequency_number: he still has generalised tonic-clonic seizures 3 nights per week
